title: Postnatal Bacterial Infections
authors: Mukherjee D, Ryan RM / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเชี่ยวชาญ Postnatal Bacterial Infections?
(1) Neonatal sepsis = major cause of morbidity + mortality worldwide: EOS incidence 0.77–1.08/1000 live births (US) — ELBW mortality 40–70%; (2) E. coli กำลัง overtake GBS เป็น #1 EOS pathogen (NRN 2015–2017: E. coli 37% vs GBS 30% — E. coli mortality 23–32% vs GBS 6–7%); (3) EOS sepsis calculator (Kaiser Permanente) ลด antibiotic use โดยไม่เพิ่ม missed EOS cases — endorsed by AAP + CDC 2019; (4) Meningitis mortality ↓ จาก 50% (1970s) → 10–15% ปัจจุบัน แต่ neurologic sequelae ยังสูง 24–29% severe; (5) Congenital syphilis เพิ่มขึ้น 477% ตั้งแต่ 2012 ถึง 2019 ใน US — ต้องรู้ workup + treatment algorithm; (6) Antibiotic stewardship สำคัญมาก — empiric antibiotics >5 วันโดยไม่มี culture ↑ NEC + candidemia + mortality ใน preterm.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
แยกประเภท EOS vs LOS: นิยาม, epidemiology, pathogenesis, risk factors, และ microbiology ที่ต่างกัน
อธิบาย clinical presentation ของ neonatal sepsis และ biomarkers ที่ใช้ได้ (CBC, CRP, procalcitonin, HeRO score)
เปรียบเทียบ 3 strategies ของ EOS risk stratification: multivariate calculator, categorical (CDC 2010), enhanced observation — และ indication แต่ละอัน
เลือก empiric antibiotics ที่ถูกต้องสำหรับ EOS vs LOS vs meningitis — และ duration ตาม organism + site
วินิจฉัยและรักษา bacterial infections by organ system: meningitis, pneumonia, UTI, osteomyelitis/septic arthritis, ophthalmia neonatorum, omphalitis
ระบุ GBS: serotypes, IAP protocol, screening timing (36–37 6/7 wk), prevention impact, late-onset GBS ที่ IAP ไม่ช่วย
จัดการ S. aureus (MSSA vs MRSA): diagnosis, vancomycin vs nafcillin, decolonization controversy, SSSS
ทำ workup และ treat congenital syphilis: RPR/VDRL algorithm, penicillin dosing, follow-up
รู้จัก Listeria, TB, botulism, tetanus ในบริบทนีโอนาทัล: source, presentation, treatment (BIG-IV, anti-TB regimen)
ประยุกต์ใช้ antibiotic stewardship: 'Choosing Wisely' ใน NICU — stop rules, duration ลด harm
1. Neonatal Sepsis — Overview, Epidemiology, Microbiology
1.1 EOS vs LOS — Definition and Pathogenesis
EOS (Early-Onset Sepsis): ≤72 hours of life; vertical transmission — in utero (amniotic fluid, PROM) หรือ intrapartum (maternal genitourinary flora); main pathogens: GBS + E. coli

LOS (Late-Onset Sepsis): >72 hours; acquisition จาก environment (caregivers, NICU surfaces, gut microbiome); main pathogens: CoNS + S. aureus + GBS (late-onset); LMIC: gram-negative Enterobacterales predominate
EOS risk factors — Maternal: GBS colonization, GBS bacteriuria, intra-amniotic infection (IAI: temp >39°C หรือ 38–39°C + leukocytosis/purulent discharge/fetal tachycardia), PROM, inadequate IAP
EOS risk factors — Neonatal: prematurity, low birth weight, congenital anomalies, instrument-assisted delivery; preterm immune impairment: ↓ monocytes/neutrophils, ↓ cytokines, ↓ T-cell activation
1.2 Epidemiology — Key Numbers
1.3 Pathogens — Current Landscape
EOS overall: E. coli surpassing GBS as #1; NRN cohort 2015–2017: E. coli 36.6% vs GBS 30.2% (Stoll JAMA Pediatr 2020)
EOS preterm: E. coli #1 (51%); GBS second (13–20%)
EOS term: GBS still dominates; Enterococcus + viridans streptococci also common
80% of E. coli EOS isolates are ampicillin resistant; overall 8% EOS pathogens resistant to both ampicillin + gentamicin
LOS: CoNS + S. aureus most common; GBS late-onset remains at 0.28–0.31/1000; MRSA outbreaks increasing in NICUs
Congenital heart disease post-surgery: CoNS, S. aureus, E. coli, Candida — ↑ mortality risk in this subgroup
2. Clinical Presentation + Diagnosis
2.1 Clinical Signs
Signs nonspecific: temperature instability, tachycardia, tachypnea, apnea, respiratory distress, lethargy, irritability, hypotonia, poor feeding, poor perfusion, abdominal distension, bleeding
Tachycardia + respiratory distress = most common presenting features; poor feeding อาจเป็น only sign ตอนแรก
Metabolic: hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis (most common), hyperbilirubinemia
Noninfectious mimics: RDS, cardiogenic edema, MAS, metabolic acidosis, electrolyte/endocrine disorders, congenital anomalies (CDH, TEF, choanal atresia, CHD)
2.2 Diagnostic Workup

Blood culture: gold standard — minimum 1 mL (reliable detection ≥1–10 CFU/mL); GBS requires ≥1 mL; automated CO2 detectors + antimicrobial neutralization elements; skin prep: chlorhexidine (CDC recommends vs betadine for late preterm/term)

Lumbar puncture: should be performed in any suspected sepsis (defer if critically unstable); meningitis in absence of bacteremia: 1–2/100,000 live births; VLBW meningitis 17-fold higher than term; poor correlation blood vs CSF culture results — LP cannot be replaced by blood culture alone

Urine culture: NOT part of EOS workup in infants ≤72h (UTI rare, hematogenous seeding primary); required for LOS (ascending infection >72h); obtain by suprapubic aspiration or sterile catheter — NOT bag specimen
2.3 Biomarkers

CBC: obtain 6–12h after birth (more reflective of pathologic response than at birth); leukopenia, ↓ ANC, I:T ratio >0.2 (EOS); one study: WBC <1000, ANC <1000, I:T >0.25 → LR of 3 for EOS in <34 wk; thrombocytopenia common in both

CRP: ↑ at 6–8h post infection; peak at 24h; sensitivity lowest early → serial values useful; poor predictive value for both EOS and LOS — should NOT solely guide antibiotic start/stop (Dhudasia J Pediatr 2023)

Procalcitonin: peaks 6–8h; physiologic ↑ first 24h; better sensitivity but less specificity than CRP; NeoPIns trial (Lancet 2017): PCT-guided therapy → shorter antibiotics in infants >34 wk with suspected EOS

HeRO score (Heart Rate Observation): predictive monitoring of HR variability + decelerations by computer algorithm; ELBW infants with HeRO monitoring → ↑ survival from LOS vs routine cardiorespiratory monitoring (Moorman J Pediatr 2011)

CSF Reference Values — ค่าปกติที่ต้องจำ
Term infants: WBC median 3 cells/mm³ (IQR 1–6) ≤7 days; protein 78 mg/dL (60–100); glucose 50 mg/dL (44–56). Preterm <37 wk: WBC median 3 (1–7); protein 116 mg/dL (93–138); glucose 53 mg/dL (43–65). VLBW <1000 g: protein 162 mg/dL (115–222) ≤7 days — สูงกว่า term มาก. WBC >20–30 cells/mm³ = meningeal inflammation — แต่ neonatal meningitis เกิดได้แม้ CSF parameters ปกติ; อย่า adjust WBC for RBC ใน traumatic tap — ไม่ improve diagnostic utility (Greenberg 2008). Multiplex PCR (FDA-approved FilmArray): useful when CSF uninterpretable หรือ post-antibiotic.
3. Risk Stratification for EOS
3.1 EOS Calculator (Multivariate Risk Assessment)
Kaiser Permanente EOS calculator: neonatalsepsiscalculator.kaiserpermanente.org — endorsed by AAP (2018) + CDC

Inputs: GA at birth + highest maternal intrapartum temperature + duration of ROM + maternal GBS status + type/duration of IAP + newborn clinical condition (well-appearing / equivocal / clinical illness)

rows: Persistent NCPAP/HFNC/MV; hemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors; NE/perinatal depression; seizure; Apgar <5 at 5 min; O2 >2h to maintain SpO2 >90%
Persistent physiologic abnormality ≥4h (HR >160, RR >60, temp instability, respiratory distress without O2) OR ≥2 such abnormalities for ≥2h

blocks.text: Outcome: ↓ antibiotic use without ↑ missed EOS vs conventional management (Achten JAMA Pediatr 2019 meta-analysis); dynamic model — risk updated with changing clinical condition
Limitation: validated only in ≥35 wk; requires local EOS incidence rates; requires serial monitoring
3.2 Categorical Risk Assessment (CDC 2010) and Enhanced Observation
Categorical (CDC 2010): risk factors = signs of clinical illness OR maternal temp ≥38°C OR inadequate IAP in GBS+ mother → blood culture + empiric antibiotics; still widely used
Enhanced observation (3rd option): ALL infants close serial clinical assessment; empiric antibiotics only if illness develops — requires institution-specific criteria + intense monitoring

Preterm risk stratification: Low-risk preterm (born for obstetric indication, no labor, no PROM, no fever/fetal distress) = clinical observation ± culture; high-risk preterm (PPROM, chorioamnionitis, preterm labor) = empiric antibiotics
4. Management — Antibiotics
4.1 EOS Empiric Therapy
Standard: Ampicillin + aminoglycoside (gentamicin) — covers GBS + E. coli; >90% susceptibility ถึงแม้ 80% E. coli ampicillin-resistant
No 3rd-gen cephalosporins for routine EOS: ↑ resistance development + ↑ invasive candidiasis (Cotton Pediatrics 2006) — avoid unless gram-negative meningitis suspected

Gram-negative meningitis suspected: add cefotaxime or ceftazidime (excellent CNS penetration; prefer cefotaxime over ceftriaxone in neonates — ceftriaxone displaces bilirubin + causes biliary sludging)
4.2 LOS Empiric Therapy
Standard: nafcillin + aminoglycoside or 3rd-gen cephalosporin — covers CoNS, S. aureus, gram-negative

Vancomycin replaces nafcillin if: preterm/complex medical problems, prior MRSA colonization/infection, high local MRSA rates — use cephalosporins (not aminoglycosides) with vancomycin to avoid renal injury
Consider empiric antifungal (Candida): severe sepsis unresponsive to antibiotics, chronic TPN, prolonged cephalosporins, unexplained cytopenia/hyperglycemia/hypertriglyceridemia
4.3 Duration

rows: 10 days; 10–14 days if <32 wk or gram-negative
14 days after 1st negative CSF culture; min 21 days uncomplicated
21 days or 14 days after 1st negative CSF, whichever longer
7–10 days IV; can switch oral if improving

blocks.text: 4.4 Antibiotic Stewardship
Stop rule: discontinue if cultures negative at 36–48h in well-appearing infants without clinical/hematologic evidence of infection

Harm of prolonged empiric antibiotics (>5 days): ↑ NEC, ↑ candidemia, ↑ mortality in premature infants (Cordero 2003, Bailey JAMA Pediatr 2014: early AB exposure → early childhood obesity via microbiome alteration)
CDC 'Choosing Wisely' campaign: sepsis calculators + early stopping rules to ↓ unnecessary antibiotics (Ho Pediatrics 2015)
5. Prevention
5.1 GBS Prevention — IAP Protocol

ACOG 2020 + AAP 2019: screen ALL pregnant women (regardless of planned mode of birth) at 36 0/7 – 37 6/7 weeks gestation; unless prior GBS bacteriuria or previous infant with GBS disease (automatic IAP)
GBS+ women: IV penicillin G (drug of choice) or ampicillin during labor; women with penicillin allergy: vancomycin or clindamycin (if susceptible)
C-section before labor onset + intact membranes: NO IAP needed even if GBS+
Impact: ↓ EOS GBS 80% (from 1.5/1000 → 0.3–0.4/1000 after 2002); NO impact on late-onset GBS (remains ~0.30/1000) — IAP does not sterilize maternal GI reservoir; vaccines under investigation
5.2 LOS Prevention
Hand hygiene + central catheter care bundles + appropriate antibiotic use + limit H2-blockers/PPIs (↑ gram-negative LOS risk)

Human milk within 72h of birth → ↓ LOS incidence; probiotics: conflicting data for LOS (↓ NEC more clear); IVIG: no benefit; routine chlorhexidine skin decontamination: NOT recommended (↓ commensal flora, alters skin pH)
6. Infections by Organ System
6.1 Bacterial Meningitis
Incidence: 0.3/1000 developed countries; 0.8–6.1/1000 developing nations; EOS meningitis: GBS most common term (40%); gram-negative bacilli 30–40% (E. coli ~50% of gram-negative); VLBW: E. coli #1

Special organisms: Citrobacter koseri + Enterobacter sakazakii → brain abscesses → must obtain brain imaging; L. monocytogenes → rhombencephalitis; CoNS/Candida/resistant gram-neg = late-onset/nosocomial
Clinical: temperature instability (60%); irritability, poor feeding, apneas, hypo/hypertonia, seizures, abnormal pupillary response; fontanelle usually full but not bulging
CSF criteria: WBC >20–30 cells/mm³; but meningitis can occur with NORMAL CSF — overlap exists; repeat LP at 24–48h if uncertain; traumatic tap: do NOT adjust WBC for RBC

Treatment: GBS: ampicillin + aminoglycoside → penicillin monotherapy after CSF sterile; gram-negative: cephalosporin 3rd-gen 21 days or 14 days post-first-negative culture; Citrobacter/Enterobacter/Pseudomonas: cefepime or meropenem (meropenem may ↑ seizure risk at lower GA); L. monocytogenes/Enterococcus: NOT covered by cephalosporins → ampicillin + aminoglycoside

Outcome: mortality ↓ from 50% (1970s) → 10–15%; survivors: 21–38% mild deficits, 24–29% severe sequelae; predictors poor outcome: seizures >72h, coma, hypotension, leukopenia, abnormal EEG; audiology testing prior to discharge + ongoing monitoring until school age
6.2 Neonatal Pneumonia

EOS pneumonia: usually congenital pneumonia — presents at birth/within 48h via aspiration of infected amniotic fluid; GBS most common in developed countries; others: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, S. aureus, E. coli, Klebsiella

Special pathogens: Ureaplasma urealyticum → chronic lung disease; azithromycin effective vs Ureaplasma + ↓ BPD (Viscardi Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal 2020); Chlamydia trachomatis: onset 2–4 wk age, staccato cough, eosinophilia; B. pertussis: apneas in preterm, doubled to 120/100,000 in early 2000s → Tdap in all pregnant women → ↓ to 60/100,000 since 2015
CXR: bilateral alveolar densities + air bronchograms or patchy infiltrates; pleural effusion in 2/3 (almost never in uncomplicated RDS); normal CXR in 15% pneumonia

Treatment: ampicillin + aminoglycoside for early-onset; vancomycin + cefotaxime for late-onset (CoNS/MRSA coverage); if Pseudomonas: aminoglycoside + antipseudomonal beta-lactam (ceftazidime/cefepime/pip-tazo); duration 5–7 days uncomplicated, longer if preterm/vented
6.3 Urinary Tract Infection
Incidence: 0.1–1% term; ~2% preterm; more common in males in neonatal period (↑ bacterial adherence to foreskin + urogenital colonization; uncircumcised > circumcised)

Pathogenesis: hematogenous spread (1/3 have concurrent bacteremia) OR ascending infection (especially if anatomic abnormality); urinary tract abnormalities in 20–50% (most common: VUR — 4x higher with Klebsiella UTI vs E. coli)

E. coli = 80% of neonatal UTIs; uropathogenic E. coli: invades + replicates within uroepithelial cells, adhesins + fimbriae → persistence; others: Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Proteus, Enterococcus faecalis

Important association: UTI ↔ conjugated hyperbilirubinemia — AAP recommends screen all infants with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia for UTI; also screen unexplained unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (E. coli most common)
Diagnosis: catheter culture ≥50,000 CFU/mL; suprapubic aspiration: any growth significant; blood culture (1/3 bacteremia); LP threshold very low (1–2% concomitant meningitis)
Imaging: renal US all neonates with UTI (structural abnormalities); VCUG at 3–6 wk post-treatment to detect VUR; if renal damage on US: cortical scintigraphy for scarring
6.4 Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

Pathophysiology unique to neonates: metaphyseal vessels make hairpin turns → sluggish flow → infection seeding; transphyseal vessels connect metaphysis to epiphysis (until 8–18 months) → osteomyelitis → epiphyseal damage + joint infection (rare in older children)

Most common bones: femur + tibia (50%), humerus, fibula; most common organisms: S. aureus + E. coli + GBS; late-onset GBS → proximal humerus osteomyelitis (especially post-shoulder dystocia); skull osteomyelitis after cephalohematoma/fetal scalp electrodes; calcaneal osteomyelitis after heel punctures (CoNS/S. aureus)
Signs: pseudoparalysis (lack of use of extremity), pain with passive motion (diaper change), joint/limb swelling; often multifocal

Imaging: plain films: soft tissue swelling earliest (48h); bony changes delayed (7–10 days); US: subperiosteal collections + joint effusions; bone scintigraphy: multiple foci; MRI: gold standard — changes within 24–48h, no radiation, best growth plate + soft tissue detail
Treatment: vancomycin + aminoglycoside/3rd-gen cephalosporin empiric; hip/shoulder joint: surgical drainage; knee/ankle/elbow: aspiration; C. osteomyelitis: 4–6 wk (MRSA 8 wk, Candida 6–12 months)
6.5 Ophthalmia Neonatorum
Definition: conjunctivitis in first month of life; causes: S. aureus, H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, enteric gram-neg, GBS, N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis, HSV, chemical

N. gonorrhoeae: perinatal transmission 30–40% of active maternal cervical infection; onset 2–5 days; profuse purulent discharge; untreated → corneal ulceration + visual loss; treat: single dose IM/IV ceftriaxone (cefotaxime if infant on IV calcium — precipitation risk); eyes irrigate with sterile saline until discharge clears

C. trachomatis: 20% of infants born to mothers with active cervical infection; onset 5–14 days ('inclusion conjunctivitis'); may progress to pneumonia 2–8 wk; treat: oral erythromycin base/succinate 14 days or azithromycin 3 days; note: erythromycin → ↑ infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis — follow up
WHO: eye prophylaxis all newborns — 0.5% erythromycin ointment or 1% tetracycline ointment (silver nitrate not available US)
6.6 Omphalitis

Incidence: <1% developed countries; 6x higher home births vs hospital; risk: LBW, PROM, umbilical catheterization, improper cord care, leukocyte adhesion defect (presents as omphalitis in neonatal period)
Pathogens: S. aureus, group A streptococci, GBS, gram-negative bacilli (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas), polymicrobial, anaerobes (if chorioamnionitis background)
Presentation: periumbilical erythema → purulent drainage + induration + tenderness (day 3–5 preterm, day 5–9 term); foul-smelling discharge → suspect anaerobes

Red flags for necrotizing fasciitis: black eschar, extensive edema, abdominal wall involvement — surgical emergency; complications: peritonitis, intra-abdominal abscess, portal vein suppurative thrombophlebitis

Treatment: antistaphylococcal penicillin (nafcillin) + aminoglycoside; vancomycin if MRSA prevalent; add clindamycin/metronidazole if anaerobes suspected (foul smell/maternal chorioamnionitis); duration 10 days; Prevention: WHO dry cord care in high-resource settings; 4% chlorhexidine to cord in low-resource settings → ↓ omphalitis + neonatal mortality
7. Specific Pathogens
7.1 Group B Streptococcus (GBS)

Serotypes: Ia, Ib, II, III, IV, V >95% EOS; >97% LOS; serotype III strongly linked to meningitis + significant LOS; GBS = most common EOS in term infants (~50% US; NRN 2015–17 ranked 2nd behind E. coli for all GAs combined)
IAP impact: ↓ EOS GBS 80%; late-onset GBS: ↑ ~4/1000 VLBW; 0.30/1000 overall — UNCHANGED by IAP; late-onset GBS mortality: 20% preterm <28 wk, 1–2% term

Treatment: penicillin G (narrow spectrum, uniformly susceptible); combo penicillin + aminoglycoside for meningitis (in vitro synergy, ↑ killing); after CSF sterile → penicillin monotherapy; erythromycin resistance 30%, clindamycin 20%; dosing varies by GA + postnatal age (see AAP Red Book Table)
Vaccines: serotype-specific polysaccharide-protein conjugate for pregnant women — under trial (WHO priority area); potential to avoid IAP resistance emergence
7.2 Staphylococcus aureus / MRSA
MRSA in NICUs: outbreaks increasing; African Americans: almost 2x invasive MRSA rate (29.9 vs 17.7/100,000 population); late-onset pattern; associated with central catheters, umbilical catheters
SSSS (Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome): exfoliative exotoxins → generalized erythematous blistering; can be fatal in neonates (lack protective antibodies) vs mild in older children

Treatment: MSSA → nafcillin; MRSA → vancomycin; clindamycin: bacteriostatic → NOT for bacteremia/severe infection; linezolid alternative; rifampin for CoNS/MSSA catheter infections (adult RCTs show no benefit); central catheter removal if possible → antibiotics ≥14 days

Skin/soft tissue: topical mupirocin for localized pustulosis (term infants); VLBW/preterm localized disease → IV; systemic signs → full sepsis evaluation + IV antibiotics; oral completion: cephalexin, clindamycin, linezolid, amoxicillin-clavulanate
Decolonization: safety + efficacy of intranasal mupirocin NOT established <12 years; proven prevention: hand washing + contact precautions + cohorting MRSA+ patients
7.3 Congenital Syphilis

Congenital Syphilis — 477% ↑ จาก 2012–2019 ใน US
Incidence 2019: 48.5 cases/100,000 live births vs 8.4/100,000 in 2012; parallels ↑ primary/secondary syphilis in women age 15–44. Transmission: transplacental any trimester/stage; higher in primary/secondary disease (60–100%) vs late latent (<8%). Clinical: hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, rash, mucocutaneous lesions, 'snuffles' (nasal secretions — highly infectious), hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, osteochondritis/periostitis, hydrops, stillbirth. Late (>2 years untreated): Hutchinson triad = interstitial keratitis + 8th cranial nerve deafness + Hutchinson teeth; saddle nose, frontal bossing, shin bowing. Screening: all pregnant women early pregnancy + at delivery; RPR/VDRL → confirm positive with FTA-ABS/TP-EIA/TP-PA ('reverse algorithm' increasingly used).
Full workup if any of: maternal RPR titer ↑ fourfold at delivery; infant RPR/VDRL ≥4x maternal titer; mother untreated or treated <4 wk before delivery; abnormal infant exam
Workup: RPR/VDRL, CBC, CSF (cell count, protein, quantitative VDRL); long bone X-ray, eye exam, LFTs, neuroimaging, hearing test, CXR — as clinically indicated

Treatment: IV penicillin G — dose by chronologic age (not GA): 50,000 U/kg every 12h if ≤1 week; every 8h if >1 week; total 10 days; procaine penicillin IM 10 days if IV access impossible; if >1 day missed → restart entire course
Follow-up: nontreponemal test every 2–3 months → should be nonreactive by 6 months if treated; if ↑ or stable titer at 6–12 months → re-evaluate + re-treat; neurosyphilis: repeat CSF every 6 months
7.4 Listeriosis

Source: L. monocytogenes — deli meats, soft/raw cheese, raw milk, enoki mushrooms, packaged salads; intracellular pathogen; crosses placenta via internalin-E-cadherin interaction on syncytiotrophoblasts → fetal infection
EOS listeriosis: transplacental; meconium in utero often associated; presentations: sepsis, pneumonia, granulomatosis infantisepticum (erythematous papules = granulomas); case fatality EOS 14–56%
LOS listeriosis: meningitis most common; insidious onset; case fatality LOS 25%

Treatment: ampicillin + gentamicin (synergy); cephalosporins have NO activity against Listeria — key distinction; after clinical improvement → ampicillin alone; meningitis 21–28 days; repeat LP 1–2 days after start to confirm CSF sterile; neuroimaging near end of treatment; discontinue iron supplements during treatment (iron ↑ Listeria pathogenicity)
7.5 Tuberculosis
Congenital TB (transplacental) vs neonatal TB (postnatal inhalation/ingestion); TB during pregnancy → ↑ preterm labor, LBW, perinatal death

Congenital TB symptoms nonspecific: respiratory distress, lethargy, fever, LBW, hepatosplenomegaly, poor feeding; TST often nonreactive in active disease — also obtain IGRA (low sensitivity but do both)

Workup: CXR; mycobacterial stains/cultures from 3 consecutive early-morning gastric aspirates (pulmonary TB); evaluate other sites if extrapulmonary TB; CSF for mycobacterial stains if LP done; examine placenta for granulomas + mycobacterial culture
Treatment: isoniazid + rifampin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol or aminoglycoside (amikacin/kanamycin/streptomycin) × 2 months → isoniazid + rifampin × ≥4 months; add corticosteroids if TB meningitis

Asymptomatic infant of mother with active TB: separate until mother on treatment + infant on isoniazid; breastfeeding OK if mother on treatment ≥2 weeks + not contagious; isoniazid until 9 months total if TST remains negative
7.6 Infant Botulism

Source: Clostridium botulinum spores — honey (classic), but most cases: environmental dust/soil; California >50% of US cases; median age: 17 weeks (range 1–60 wk); ~100–150 US cases/year (75% infant botulism)
Mechanism: spores → colonize GI tract → toxin produced in vivo → irreversible blockade of acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junction → descending symmetric flaccid paralysis
Classic triad: hypotonia + constipation + poor feeding; lethargy, drooling, cranial nerve palsies, weak cry, ↓ DTRs, loss of gag/swallow/suck → airway compromise; typically afebrile
Diagnosis: stool for C. botulinum spores + toxin (send to state health dept or CDC — takes days); EMG: short-duration low-amplitude motor unit potentials + abnormal incremental response

Treatment: BIG-IV (BabyBIG) — Botulism Immune Globulin IV (human-derived antitoxin); RCT: ↓ hospitalization, ICU, MV, tube feeds (Arnon NEJM 2006); give ASAP; NO antibiotics — lysis of C. botulinum → ↑ toxin release; supportive care; case fatality <1%; most recover fully (weeks)
7.7 Neonatal Tetanus

Clostridium tetani spores → germinate → tetanospasmin → binds neuromuscular synapse irreversibly → ↑ muscle tone + spasms + autonomic instability; ↓ 96% deaths since 1980s (WHO); ~34,019 deaths 2015 — mainly inadequate maternal immunization + unhygienic birth

Clinical: onset ~1 week (range 1 day – 1 month); irritability, poor suck, fever, rigidity, muscle spasms (exacerbated by stimulation), hyperreflexia, extreme toe flexion; trismus less common in infants; tachycardia, apnea, laryngospasm → aspiration pneumonia

Treatment: IM human tetanus immune globulin (neutralize unbound toxin); metronidazole (↓ toxin-producing bacteria); airway management + ventilation essential; benzodiazepines + baclofen for spasms; MgSO4 + beta-blockers for autonomic dysfunction; minimize stimulation (precipitates spasms); mortality: <20% with modern ICU; <50% with basic medications + nursing care
8. GBS Antibiotic Dosing Reference
*Ampicillin/PenG dosed more frequently in infants >7 days due to pharmacokinetic differences in drug clearance. Refer to AAP Red Book 2021–2024 for full dosing table.
9. NICU Practical — Clinical Decision Framework
9.1 EOS Decision Algorithm
≥35 wk: use EOS calculator (dynamic model, re-assess with clinical change) OR categorical risk approach OR enhanced observation — institutional choice; calculator ↓ antibiotic use

Preterm <35 wk: low-risk preterm (obstetric indication, no labor, no PROM, no fever) → observe ± culture; high-risk preterm (PPROM, chorioamnionitis, preterm labor, fetal distress) → empiric antibiotics + blood culture

Culture-negative at 36–48h + well-appearing: stop antibiotics regardless of CRP/PCT alone — 'serial abnormal CRP/PCT should NOT be used to guide antibiotic duration in absence of positive culture until larger trials done'
9.2 When to Think Congenital Syphilis
ANY infant with: hepatosplenomegaly + thrombocytopenia + unexplained bony pain (pseudoparalysis)/rash/mucocutaneous lesions/nasal secretions → full workup
Always check maternal RPR/VDRL at delivery — if mother not tested → test mother + obtain infant RPR/VDRL + full workup if any maternal risk factor
Remember: congenital syphilis rate rising rapidly — maintain high index of suspicion regardless of country of origin
9.3 Approach to Suspected Meningitis
Do LP before antibiotics if infant clinically stable; defer LP if respiratory/hemodynamic instability → start antibiotics + defer LP until stable
CSF Gram stain + culture + cell count/differential + protein + glucose → multiplex PCR if post-antibiotic or uninterpretable CSF
Brain imaging if: Citrobacter or E. sakazakii isolated (brain abscess!); delayed clinical response; prolonged positive CSF cultures; seizures; slow clinical response
Audiology prior to discharge + follow-up for all confirmed meningitis
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — E. coli กำลัง Overtake GBS: Ampicillin Alone ไม่เพียงพอ
NRN 2015–2017 (Stoll JAMA Pediatr 2020): E. coli 36.6% vs GBS 30.2% ของ all-GA EOS. 80% E. coli EOS isolates are ampicillin-resistant. E. coli mortality 23–32% vs GBS 6–7%. ดังนั้น: standard empiric regimen = ampicillin + gentamicin ครอบคลุมเพียงพอ (>90% susceptibility overall) แต่ถ้าสงสัย gram-negative meningitis → เพิ่ม cefotaxime (NOT ceftriaxone — displaces bilirubin + biliary sludging). อย่าลืม: E. coli meningitis = min 21 days or 14 days post-negative CSF แล้วแต่อันไหนนานกว่า.

PEARL 2 — EOS Calculator ≥35 wk: ↓ Antibiotics, ไม่ ↑ Missed Cases
Achten JAMA Pediatr 2019 (meta-analysis): widespread EOS calculator use → significant ↓ in empiric antibiotic administration without ↑ missed EOS vs conventional categorical approach. Calculator is dynamic — re-assess clinical condition and update risk at any time. Validated ≥35 wk; requires local EOS incidence. Three options: (1) calculator, (2) CDC 2010 categorical, (3) enhanced observation — all AAP-endorsed. For preterm <35 wk: risk-stratify separately — low-risk preterm (no chorioamnionitis/no PROM/no labor) can be observed ± culture.

PEARL 3 — Prolonged Empiric Antibiotics Cause Harm
Empiric antibiotics >5 days ใน preterm without positive culture → ↑ NEC (Cordero 2003), ↑ candidemia (Cotton 2006), ↑ mortality. Bailey JAMA Pediatr 2014: early antibiotic exposure → altered microbiome → ↑ childhood obesity. CDC 'Choosing Wisely': stop empiric antibiotics if: blood culture negative 36–48h + clinically well. ใน equivocal cases: อย่าใช้ serial CRP/PCT alone to continue antibiotics — poor predictive value (Dhudasia J Pediatr 2023). Prescribe, reevaluate, de-escalate.

PEARL 4 — Congenital Syphilis: ต้อง Test แม่ทุกคนที่ Delivery
US 2019: 48.5 cases/100,000 live births — 477% ↑ จาก 2012. Penicillin = ONLY treatment for pregnant women. Algorithm: maternal RPR/VDRL → confirm with FTA-ABS/TP-EIA. Workup infant if: maternal titer ↑ 4-fold, infant titer ≥4x mother, untreated/inadequately treated mother, abnormal infant exam. Treatment: IV penicillin G 50,000 U/kg — dosing frequency depends on chronologic age (not GA). If >1 day missed → restart entire 10-day course. Follow-up nontreponemal titer every 2–3 months — should be nonreactive by 6 months.

PEARL 5 — Infant Botulism: ห้ามให้ Antibiotics, ให้ BIG-IV เร็วที่สุด
Classic triad: hypotonia + constipation + poor feeding. Typically afebrile. Descending symmetric paralysis. Source: honey (avoid in infants <1 year) + environmental dust/spores. Treatment: BIG-IV (BabyBIG) ASAP — Arnon NEJM 2006 RCT: ↓ hospital stay, MV, tube feeds. Antibiotics CONTRAINDICATED — lysis of C. botulinum in GI tract → ↑ toxin release. Stool cultures (sent to state lab) confirm diagnosis but take days — do not wait for results before giving BIG-IV. Contact California Infant Botulism Treatment Program: 510-231-7600 / infantbotulism.org. Case fatality <1% with modern care.
11. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Stoll BJ et al JAMA Pediatr 2020;174:e200593: NRN 2015–2017: E. coli now 36.6% vs GBS 30.2% of all EOS — E. coli surpassing GBS; E. coli mortality 23–32% vs GBS 6–7%
Nanduri SA et al JAMA Pediatr 2019;173:224: GBS EOS ↓ from 0.37 to 0.23/1000 (2006–2015); late-onset GBS stable at ~0.30/1000 — IAP no impact on LOS
Flannery DD et al Pediatrics 2021;148: EOS among very preterm infants: E. coli #1 pathogen in VLBW EOS (51%); mortality inversely proportional to GA
Flannery DD et al Pediatrics 2022;150: LOS VLBW: overall 8.9%; rate 32% in infants born at 23 wk; CoNS + S. aureus most common LOS pathogens
Achten NB et al JAMA Pediatr 2019;173:1032: meta-analysis EOS calculator: significant ↓ in antibiotic administration without ↑ missed EOS — endorsed as primary risk stratification tool
Puopolo KM et al Pediatrics 2018;142:e20182894: AAP clinical report: management of neonates ≥35 0/7 wk with suspected EOS — endorsed EOS calculator; endorsed by CDC
Puopolo KM et al Pediatrics 2019;144: AAP clinical report: management of infants at risk for GBS — IAP, screening algorithm; endorsed by ACOG + CDC
Stocker M et al Lancet 2017;390:871 (NeoPIns): procalcitonin-guided antibiotic therapy → shorter treatment duration in neonates >34 wk with suspected EOS — multicenter RCT
Dhudasia MB et al J Pediatr 2023;256:98: poor diagnostic performance of CRP for EOS — should not solely guide antibiotic start/stop
Moorman JR et al J Pediatr 2011;159:900: HeRO score monitoring VLBW → ↑ survival from LOS vs routine cardiorespiratory monitoring; RCT
Cotton CM et al Pediatrics 2006;118:717: 3rd-gen cephalosporin use ↔ ↑ invasive candidiasis in ELBW — avoid as empiric EOS therapy
Cordero L, Ayers LW Infect Control Hosp Epidemiol 2003;24:662: prolonged empiric antibiotics >5 days in ELBW without infection → ↑ mortality; early stop rules needed
Bailey LC et al JAMA Pediatr 2014;168:1063: early infant antibiotic exposure → altered microbiome → ↑ early childhood obesity — another reason for stewardship
Arnon SS et al NEJM 2006;354:462: BIG-IV (BabyBIG) RCT: ↓ hospital stay, ICU days, MV, tube/IV feedings; no serious adverse events — standard of care for infant botulism
Viscardi RM et al Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2020;105:615: RCT azithromycin vs placebo for Ureaplasma in preterm: effective + ↓ BPD in colonized infants
CDC Congenital Syphilis 2023: 477% ↑ from 2012 to 2019 — public health emergency; penicillin remains only treatment; all pregnant women screen at first prenatal visit + delivery
Greenberg RG et al Pediatr Infect Dis J 2008;27:1047: traumatic LP: do not adjust CSF WBC for RBC — does not improve diagnostic utility for neonatal meningitis
Ku LC et al Clin Perinatol 2015;42:29: bacterial meningitis in infants: mortality ↓ from 50% to 10–15%; 24–29% severe neurologic sequelae; audiology monitoring essential
Ho T et al Pediatrics 2015;136:e482: 'Choosing Wisely' in newborn medicine: 5 opportunities including ↓ unnecessary sepsis evaluations + ↓ antibiotic duration
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: EOS กับ LOS ต่างกันอย่างไรในด้าน definition, pathogenesis, และ pathogens?

ตอบ: EOS: ≤72h; vertical transmission (in utero/intrapartum); GBS + E. coli (E. coli now #1 in preterm + all-GA NRN 2015-17). LOS: >72h; environmental acquisition (gut microbiome, caregivers, surfaces); CoNS + S. aureus most common; LMIC: gram-negative Enterobacterales. EOS mortality = inversely proportional to GA: ELBW 40–70% vs term 2.5–3%. E. coli mortality 23–32% vs GBS 6–7% (Stoll JAMA Pediatr 2020).
Q: EOS calculator ใช้ข้อมูลอะไรบ้าง? ข้อดีและข้อจำกัดคืออะไร?

ตอบ: Inputs: GA + highest maternal intrapartum temp + duration of ROM + GBS status + type/duration IAP + clinical exam (well/equivocal/clinical illness). ข้อดี: ↓ antibiotic use without ↑ missed EOS; dynamic model (Achten JAMA Pediatr 2019 meta-analysis). ข้อจำกัด: validated ≥35 wk only; requires local EOS incidence; needs serial clinical monitoring. สำหรับ preterm <35 wk: low-risk (obstetric indication, no labor, no PROM, no fever) = observe ± culture; high-risk (PPROM, chorioamnionitis, preterm labor) = empiric antibiotics.
Q: ทำไมต้องระวัง ceftriaxone ในทารกแรกเกิด? จะใช้อะไรแทน?

ตอบ: Ceftriaxone: displaces bilirubin from albumin → ↑ kernicterus risk + biliary sludging. แทนด้วย cefotaxime (excellent CNS penetration; ไม่ displace bilirubin). ข้อยกเว้น: gonococcal conjunctivitis → ceftriaxone single dose acceptable (unless infant on IV calcium — then cefotaxime เพราะ precipitation risk). สำหรับ gram-negative meningitis: cefotaxime 21 days or 14 days post-first-negative CSF.
Q: LP ควรทำก่อน หรือหลัง antibiotics? กรณีไหนที่ defer ได้?

ตอบ: Clinically stable: LP ก่อน antibiotics (CSF Gram stain + culture + multiplex PCR). Defer LP ถ้า: respiratory/hemodynamic instability → start antibiotics + defer until stable. Blood culture ลืมใช้แทน LP ไม่ได้ — poor correlation blood vs CSF cultures (25% meningitis have bacteremia; but meningitis without bacteremia also occurs). Multiplex PCR (FilmArray, FDA-approved): useful post-antibiotic or uninterpretable CSF.
Q: Congenital syphilis — ระบุ workup ที่ต้องทำและ treatment ที่ถูกต้อง

ตอบ: Workup ถ้า indicate: RPR/VDRL (infant), CBC, CSF (cell count/protein/quantitative VDRL), long bone X-ray, eye exam, LFTs, neuroimaging, hearing test, CXR. Treatment: IV penicillin G 50,000 U/kg — ≤1 wk age: q12h; >1 wk: q8h; total 10 days. Dose by chronologic age NOT GA. If >1 day missed → restart entire course. Follow-up: nontreponemal titer q2–3 mo → nonreactive by 6 months. ↑ or stable at 6–12 mo → re-evaluate + re-treat.
Q: Infant botulism — ทำไมห้ามให้ antibiotics? Treatment คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Mechanism: C. botulinum spores colonize GI → produce toxin in vivo → antibiotics lyse bacteria → ↑ toxin release → worsens paralysis. Treatment: BIG-IV (BabyBIG) ASAP — human-derived botulinum antitoxin. Arnon NEJM 2006 RCT: ↓ hospital stay, MV duration, tube feeds. Supportive care: airway management critical. Confirmatory stool test (send to state lab/CDC) takes days — treat clinically; do not wait for results. Contact CA Infant Botulism Program: 510-231-7600.
Q: omphalitis ที่ severe มี complication อะไร? Red flags ของ necrotizing fasciitis คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Complications: peritonitis, intra-abdominal abscess, suppurative thrombophlebitis (portal/umbilical veins), umbilical hernia + bowel ischemia. Necrotizing fasciitis red flags: black eschar + extensive edema + abdominal wall involvement → surgical emergency, high case fatality if delayed. Treatment: nafcillin + aminoglycoside; vancomycin if MRSA prevalent; clindamycin/metronidazole if anaerobes (foul smell/chorioamnionitis background); 10 days. Immune deficiency red flag: omphalitis ใน neonate → think leukocyte adhesion defect.
Q: Listeriosis — ทำไม cephalosporins ใช้ไม่ได้? Treatment คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Listeria monocytogenes มี intrinsic resistance ต่อ cephalosporins ทุกชนิด — เพราะ cephalosporins ไม่ bind กับ PBP ของ Listeria. Treatment: ampicillin + gentamicin (synergy in vitro + better outcomes); หลัง clinical improvement → ampicillin monotherapy; bacteremia: 14 days; meningitis: 21–28 days; repeat LP 1–2 days after start; discontinue iron supplements (iron ↑ Listeria pathogenicity); neuroimaging near end of treatment.
Q: Late-onset GBS — IAP ช่วยได้ไหม? อะไร คือ incidence และ mortality ใน preterm?

ตอบ: IAP ไม่ลด late-onset GBS — IAP sterilizes maternal genitourinary tract แต่ไม่ eradicate maternal GI reservoir; infant ยังได้รับ GBS จาก maternal GI colonization หลัง birth. Incidence: ~0.30/1000 live births (unchanged 2006–2015), ↑ ~4/1000 in VLBW (Flannery Pediatrics 2022). Mortality: preterm <28 wk: up to 20%; term: 1–2% (Nanduri JAMA Pediatr 2019). GBS vaccine = priority (WHO) — would address LOS + EOS in single intervention.
Q: CSF findings ใน VLBW infant แตกต่างจาก term infant อย่างไร? ระวังอะไรใน interpretation?

ตอบ: VLBW <1000 g: protein 162 mg/dL (115–222) ≤7 days (เทียบ term: 78 mg/dL). WBC median comparable (3–4 cells/mm³). Key: CSF WBC >20–30 = meningeal inflammation แต่ meningitis เกิดได้กับ CSF ปกติ. Traumatic tap: อย่า adjust WBC for RBC — ไม่ improve diagnostic utility (Greenberg 2008). Ultrasound-guided LP ↓ traumatic taps (Oulego-Erroz J Pediatr 2014). ถ้า CSF borderline + suspicious → repeat LP 24–48h → pleocytosis will persist with true meningitis.
Q: Neonatal UTI — เมื่อไหร่ screen? ทำไม bag specimen ไม่ได้? การ image ต้องทำอะไร?

ตอบ: Screen เฉพาะ LOS (>72h age) — UTI EOS ≤72h แทบไม่เกิด (mostly hematogenous seeding); bag specimen = contaminated → false positive → ห้ามใช้ (urine bag not acceptable for diagnosis). ใช้: catheter ≥50,000 CFU/mL = positive; suprapubic aspiration: any growth = significant. Imaging: renal US all neonates with UTI (structural); VCUG ที่ 3–6 wk post-treatment (VUR detection); cortical scintigraphy ถ้า renal damage on US (scarring); UTI ↔ conjugated hyperbilirubinemia → screen for UTI (AAP recommendation).

Day 148 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.51
บทต่อไป: Chapter 51 — Fungal and Protozoal Infections of the Neonate. เตรียมตัว: invasive candidiasis ใน NICU: risk factors (ELBW, TPN, broad-spectrum antibiotics, steroids), clinical presentation, diagnosis (blood culture sensitivity, beta-D-glucan); antifungal agents: fluconazole vs amphotericin B vs micafungin — ข้อดีข้อเสียแต่ละตัว; fluconazole prophylaxis ใน ELBW; congenital toxoplasmosis: classic triad (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications), pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine treatment; malaria ใน neonates: clinical features, diagnosis, chloroquine vs quinine; Pneumocystis jirovecii ใน immunocompromised neonates.
